segments.inferno-7-g1: This is an obscure demonic exclamation rather than normal Italian syntax. “Satàn” is a direct invocation of Satan, while “Pape” functions as a cry or interjection before the name.
segments.inferno-7-g2: The cry is repeated and intensified with “aleppe,” another obscure word often treated as infernal gibberish or a distorted learned allusion. The segment has no ordinary finite verb or grammatical sentence structure; it is a shouted formula.
segments.inferno-7-g3: The comma marks the transition from the quoted cry to the narrative verb that follows. It has no independent grammatical content, but it shows that the previous utterance is being introduced retrospectively by the coming verb “cominciò.”
segments.inferno-7-g4: “Cominciò” is passato remoto, the narrative past: “began.” The subject “Pluto” follows the verb, a common poetic and narrative order; “con la voce chioccia” means “with a hoarse voice,” with “chioccia” agreeing with feminine “voce.”
segments.inferno-7-g5: “E” links Plutus’s speech to Virgil’s response. “Quel savio gentil” is a noun phrase meaning “that noble sage,” with “savio” used substantively as “wise man/sage” and “gentil” an apocopated poetic form of “gentile.”
segments.inferno-7-g6: “Che” introduces a relative clause referring to “quel savio gentil.” “Seppe” is passato remoto of “sapere,” here meaning “knew,” and “tutto” is its object: “who knew everything.”
segments.inferno-7-g7: “Disse” is passato remoto of “dire,” continuing the main narrative action. “Per confortarmi” uses “per” plus infinitive to express purpose, and “-mi” is an enclitic object pronoun: “to comfort/encourage me.”
segments.inferno-7-g8: “Non ti noccia” is a negative jussive or optative subjunctive: literally, “may it not harm you.” The subject comes after the verb, “la tua paura,” so the sense is “let your fear not harm/trouble you.”
segments.inferno-7-g9: “Ché” is causal here, introducing the reason for Virgil’s reassurance. It means “for” or “because,” not simply the relative “that.”
segments.inferno-7-g10: This is a concessive phrase: “whatever power he may have.” “Abbia” is present subjunctive of “avere,” used after “che” in a non-factual or concessive construction; “elli” is the archaic form for “he.”
segments.inferno-7-g11: “Torrà” is future of the old verb “torre,” meaning “will take away” or “will prevent/deprive.” “Lo scender” is a substantivized infinitive, “the descending,” and “questa roccia” gives the route or object of descent: he will not prevent us from going down this rocky slope.
segments.inferno-7-g12: “Poi” advances the action: “then.” “Si rivolse” is passato remoto with a pronominal/reflexive form, meaning “he turned himself/toward,” followed by “a” for the addressee; “’nfiata” is an aphetic poetic form of “infiata,” “swollen,” modifying “labbia,” here “face” or “countenance.”
segments.inferno-7-g13: “E disse” coordinates a second narrative action with the preceding “si rivolse.” “Disse” is passato remoto, the standard tense of completed narrative events in Dante.
segments.inferno-7-g14: “Taci” is the second-person singular imperative of “tacere”: “be silent.” It is a direct command addressed to Plutus.
segments.inferno-7-g15: “Maladetto lupo” is a vocative insult: “accursed wolf.” “Maladetto” is an archaic or variant form of modern “maledetto,” and “lupo” is metaphorical for a ravenous, rapacious being rather than merely a literal animal.
segments.inferno-7-g16: “Consuma” is a second-person singular imperative; with no explicit object, it has the force of “waste away” or “consume yourself.” “Dentro te” is an archaic/poetic way to say “within yourself,” and “con la tua rabbia” gives the means or cause: by your own rage.
segments.inferno-7-g35: “Percotëansi” is an archaic/poetic form with the reciprocal pronoun attached: literally “they struck themselves/one another,” i.e. “they struck against one another.” “’ncontro” is a contracted form of “incontro,” here meaning “against,” completing the idea of collision.
segments.inferno-7-g36: “E poscia” continues the action with “and afterwards/then”; “pur lì” intensifies the place, “right there.” The verb “si rivolgea” is an imperfect singular form because its subject is the postposed singular “ciascun”: “each one turned himself/turned back.”
segments.inferno-7-g37: “Voltando” is a gerund expressing an action simultaneous with “si rivolgea”: as each turns, he is “turning around.” “A retro” is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning “backward” or “toward the rear.”
segments.inferno-7-g38: “Gridando” is another gerund linked to the same subject, “ciascun,” describing what they do while turning back: they cry out or shout. It introduces the quoted accusation that follows.
segments.inferno-7-g39: “Perché” is interrogative, “why?” “Tieni” is the second-person singular present of “tenere”; in this moral context it means “do you hoard/hold on to wealth?” rather than merely “hold.”
segments.inferno-7-g40: The initial “e” links this cry to the previous shouted question, giving the paired accusation of the opposite group. “Perché burli?” uses “perché” as “why,” and “burli” is a second-person singular present form meaning “you squander” or “you waste.”
segments.inferno-7-g41: This segment consists only of punctuation, closing the preceding direct speech and sentence. It contains no lexical items to assign senses to.
segments.inferno-7-g42: “Così” means “thus/in this way,” referring back to the repeated collision and reversal. “Tornavan” is a shortened imperfect third-person plural form of “tornavano,” describing the ongoing repeated motion; “per lo cerchio tetro” means through or around the gloomy circle, while “da ogne mano a l’opposito punto” means from each side to the opposite point.
segments.inferno-7-g43: “Gridandosi” is a gerund with reciprocal “si”: they are shouting at one another. “Anche” here has the older sense “still,” and “loro ontoso metro” means “their shameful refrain,” with “metro” used metaphorically for a repeated chant or formula.
segments.inferno-7-g44: “Poi” advances the sequence: after reaching the opposite point, the action repeats. “Si volgea” is an imperfect singular form with reflexive “si,” agreeing with the singular subject “ciascun,” “each one turned himself/turned back.”
segments.inferno-7-g45: “Quand’” is “quando” elided before the vowel of “era.” “Era giunto” uses “essere” as auxiliary with the past participle “giunto,” giving a pluperfect sense in English: “when he had arrived/reached the point.”
segments.inferno-7-g46: “Per lo suo mezzo cerchio” means along or through his own half of the circle; “lo suo” is an archaic article-plus-possessive construction. “A l’altra giostra” means “to the other joust,” a metaphor for the next clash or encounter with the opposite group.
segments.inferno-7-g26: This is an interrogative clause: chi is the subject, stipa is present indicative, and tante anticipates the correlative quant’ io viddi, meaning “as many ... as I saw.” Nove is an archaic/poetic form meaning “new” or “strange,” modifying travaglie e pene; viddi is the old first-person singular past tense of vedere, “I saw.”
segments.inferno-7-g27: The sentence continues the lament with e perché, “and why,” introducing a direct question. Nostra colpa is the subject, sì is an intensifier meaning “so,” and ne is an archaic object pronoun here meaning “us,” while scipa is present indicative, “ruins” or “destroys.”
segments.inferno-7-g28: Come introduces a simile, “as,” and fa is elliptical: “as the wave does/acts.” Là sovra Cariddi places the image over or above Charybdis, the famous whirlpool of classical geography and myth.
segments.inferno-7-g29: Che is a relative pronoun referring to onda. Si frange is a reflexive or middle construction, “breaks itself/breaks,” and con quella means “against that one,” namely another wave; in cui s’intoppa means “into/with which it collides,” with s’ as elided si before a vowel.
segments.inferno-7-g30: Così completes the comparison begun by come: “so/thus.” Convien che introduces a necessary consequence, “it is necessary that,” followed by the subjunctive riddi, from riddare, meaning that the crowd must circle or whirl round here.
segments.inferno-7-g31: Qui is fronted for emphasis, followed by vid’ i’, an elided form of vidi io, “I saw.” Gente is a feminine singular collective noun, so troppa agrees with it; più ch’altrove forms a comparative, “more than elsewhere,” with ch’ standing for che in the sense “than.”
segments.inferno-7-g32: E d’una parte e d’altra is a balanced correlative expression, literally “both from one side and from the other.” The elided d’ is di, here indicating origin or position: the sinners are arranged or coming from opposite sides.
segments.inferno-7-g33: Con grand’ urli is an instrumental or accompanying phrase, “with great howls.” Grand’ is an elided form of grande before a vowel, and it intensifies the sound rather than describing physical size.
segments.inferno-7-g34: Voltando is a present participle depending on the main verb vid’ i’, describing what the people were doing: “turning” or “rolling” weights. Per forza di poppa means “by the force/strength of the chest,” with poppa used poetically for the breast or front of the body.
segments.inferno-7-g17: The nominal infinitive “l’andare” functions as the subject of “è”: literally, “the going to the depths is not without cause.” “Sanza” is an archaic form of modern “senza,” and “al cupo” uses the adjective “cupo” substantively to mean the dark/deep place.
segments.inferno-7-g18: “Vuolsi” is an impersonal/passive form, literally “it is willed,” with the enclitic pronoun “si” attached to the verb. “Ne l’alto” means “in the high place,” that is, in Heaven; the phrase gives the divine source of the command.
segments.inferno-7-g19: “Là dove” introduces a relative locative clause, “there where.” “Fé” is a contracted poetic preterite of “fece,” and “fé la vendetta” means “carried out retribution”; “del superbo strupo” identifies the offense as a proud outrage, traditionally the rebellion of Lucifer and his angels.
segments.inferno-7-g20: “Quali ... tal” forms a correlative simile: “as ... so.” The subject is “le gonfiate vele,” placed after the modifier “dal vento”; “caggiono” is an archaic present plural for “cadono,” and “avvolte” is a feminine plural participle agreeing with “vele.”
segments.inferno-7-g21: “Poi che” functions together like modern “poiché” in the temporal sense “after/when.” “L’alber” is an apocopated form of “albero,” here meaning the ship’s mast, and “fiacca” is present indicative used vividly within the simile: “the mast breaks/snaps.”
segments.inferno-7-g22: “Tal” completes the comparison begun by “Quali,” meaning “so” or “in such a way.” “Cadde” is a perfect tense form, moving from the general present-tense image of the sails to the single completed fall of “la fiera crudele,” the cruel beast.
segments.inferno-7-g23: “Così” links the action to what has just happened: “thus/so.” “Scendemmo” is first-person plural perfect, and “ne la” is the separated form of modern “nella,” here with motion into the “quarta lacca,” the fourth hollow or depression of Hell.
segments.inferno-7-g24: “Pigliando” is a gerund modifying “scendemmo,” meaning that they descended while taking or following more of the bank. “Che” introduces a relative clause referring to the “ripa” or infernal enclosure; “’l” is the elided article “il,” and “insacca” literally suggests bagging up, hence enclosing or cramming in the evil of the whole universe.
segments.inferno-7-g25: This is an exclamatory apostrophe: Dante breaks off to address or invoke divine justice directly. “Di Dio” is a possessive/genitive phrase, “of God,” specifying the justice as God’s own justice.
segments.inferno-7-g146: “Venimmo” is the first-person plural passato remoto of “venire,” used narratively: “we came” or “we reached.” “Al piè d’una torre” means “to the foot/base of a tower,” with “piè” as a shortened poetic form of “piede”; “torre” here is a noun meaning “tower,” not the verb senses listed. “Al da sezzo” is an archaic adverbial expression meaning “at last” or “finally,” literally built from prepositional elements plus “sezzo” meaning “last.”
segments.inferno-7-g76: “Or” means “now,” introducing Virgil’s conclusion from what Dante has just seen. “Puoi” is the second-person singular present of “potere,” governing the infinitive “veder” in the next segment: “you can see.”
segments.inferno-7-g77: “Figliuol” is an affectionate vocative, an apocopated form of “figliuolo,” addressed by Virgil to Dante. It stands outside the syntax of the clause, like “my son” or “child.”
segments.inferno-7-g78: “Veder” is an apocopated infinitive dependent on “puoi”: “you can see/understand.” “La corta buffa” is literally “the brief jest/farce,” and “d’i ben” means “of the goods,” with “ben” used substantively for possessions; “che son commessi a la fortuna” is a relative clause meaning that these goods are “entrusted to Fortune.”
segments.inferno-7-g79: “Per che” means “for which” or “on account of which,” referring back to worldly goods. “L’umana gente” is the subject, and “si rabbuffa” is reflexive/reciprocal in force: humankind gets itself into turmoil or wrangles over them.
segments.inferno-7-g80: “Ché” introduces the reason for the previous statement: “for/because.” “Tutto l’oro” is the subject of the larger negative clause completed in the next segment; the two relative clauses “ch’è sotto la luna” and “che già fu” mean “that is under the moon” and “that once existed.”
segments.inferno-7-g81: “Di quest’ anime stanche” is partitive with “una”: “one of these weary souls.” “Poterebbe” is an archaic/poetic conditional form for modern “potrebbe,” and “farne posare una” means “make one of them rest,” with “ne” referring back to the souls.
segments.inferno-7-g82: “Maestro mio” is a vocative phrase: Dante addresses Virgil directly as “my master.” The possessive “mio” follows the noun, a normal Italian order that also suits the elevated style.
segments.inferno-7-g83: “Diss’ io” is “dissi io,” with elision before “io”: “I said.” The verb precedes the subject pronoun, a common narrative inversion in poetry.
segments.inferno-7-g84: “Or” again means “now,” marking Dante’s request. “Mi dì” uses the imperative “dì” from “dire” with “mi” as an indirect object, “tell me,” and “anche” adds “also/further.”
segments.inferno-7-g85: “Questa fortuna” introduces the topic of Dante’s question: “this Fortune.” “Di che tu mi tocche” literally means “of which you touch to me,” an archaic way of saying “of which you speak/mention to me”; “tocche” is a poetic form corresponding to “tocchi.”
segments.inferno-7-g86: “Che è” asks “what is she/it?” with “che” functioning as an interrogative pronoun. The subject is the “fortuna” just mentioned.
segments.inferno-7-g87: Here “che” is relative, referring back to Fortune: “who/which.” “I ben del mondo” means “the goods of the world,” and “ha sì tra branche” literally says she has them “so in her claws,” meaning she holds them so firmly in her grasp.
segments.inferno-7-g88: This segment consists only of punctuation, marking the end of the preceding quoted question. It contains no words requiring lexical interpretation.
segments.inferno-7-g89: “E quelli a me” is a compact narrative formula: “And he to me,” meaning “and he said to me.” “Quelli” refers to Virgil, while “a me” supplies the indirect object of the implied verb of speaking.
segments.inferno-7-g90: “Oh creature sciocche” is a vocative exclamation addressed broadly to foolish human beings. The adjective “sciocche” agrees in feminine plural with “creature.”
segments.inferno-7-g91: “Quanta ignoranza” is an exclamatory phrase: “how much/how great ignorance.” “È quella che v’offende” literally means “is that which harms you,” with “v’” elided from “vi” before a vowel and functioning as the object of “offende.”
segments.inferno-7-g117: The subject is “L’acqua,” with “era” in the imperfect, describing the water’s continuing appearance. “Assai più che” forms a comparison, “much more than,” and “persa” is an archaic color adjective meaning a very dark purple or nearly black shade.
segments.inferno-7-g118: “E noi” begins the second main clause and sets up “we” as the subject of the later verb “intrammo.” The pronoun is explicit for emphasis and contrast after the description of the water.
segments.inferno-7-g119: This prepositional phrase is inserted between subject and verb: “in compagnia de l’onde bige” means that they move together with, or amid, the murky waves. “De l’” is the older separated form of modern “delle,” and “bige” describes the waves as grayish or muddy-colored.
segments.inferno-7-g120: “Intrammo” is first-person plural past definite of “intrare,” here “we entered.” “Giù” gives downward direction, while “per una via diversa” means along or through a different path, with “per” marking the route taken.
segments.inferno-7-g121: The normal prose order would be “questo tristo ruscel va in la palude...”: Dante places the destination first for poetic emphasis. “C’ha nome Stige” is an elided relative expression, effectively “che ha nome Stige,” meaning “which has the name Styx” or “which is called Styx.”
segments.inferno-7-g122: “Quand’ è disceso” uses “essere” plus a past participle, literally “when it is descended,” meaning “once it has flowed down.” “Al piè de le...” means “to/at the foot of the...,” with “de le” as the older separated form of “delle”; the adjectives “maligne” and “grige” modify “piagge.”
segments.inferno-7-g123: “E io” introduces Dante himself as the subject of the main verb “vidi” later in the sentence. The explicit “io” prepares for the relative clause that interrupts before the main action.
segments.inferno-7-g124: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “io”: “I, who...” The phrase “di mirare stava inteso” means “was intent on gazing”; “stava” is imperfect, and “inteso” functions adjectivally with “di” plus infinitive.
segments.inferno-7-g125: “Vidi” is the first-person singular past definite, “I saw,” resuming the main clause after the relative interruption. “Genti fangose” is the object, “muddy people,” located “in quel pantano,” in that marsh or mire.
segments.inferno-7-g126: “Ignude tutte” is an appositive description of “genti,” agreeing in feminine plural. The adjective comes before “tutte” in a poetic order: “all naked.”
segments.inferno-7-g127: This final prepositional phrase adds another descriptive detail to the people seen. “Sembiante” means their face, look, or countenance, and “offeso” describes it as injured, hurt, or wounded-looking.
segments.inferno-7-g128: “Queste” refers back to the “genti,” treated as a feminine plural group: “these people/souls.” “Si percotean” is an archaic shortened imperfect for “si percotevano,” with reciprocal “si,” meaning “they were striking one another”; “non pur” means “not merely” or “not only.”
segments.inferno-7-g129: This segment completes the correlative structure “non pur... ma...”: not only with the hand, but also with head, chest, and feet. “Col” and “coi” are contractions of “con il” and “con i,” here marking the body parts used as instruments.
segments.inferno-7-g130: “Troncandosi” is a gerund with reciprocal “si,” describing a simultaneous action: while striking, they are tearing or mutilating one another. “Co’ denti” is an archaic contraction for “con i denti,” and “a brano a brano” is an idiom meaning “piece by piece.”
segments.inferno-7-g62: “E io” introduces Dante’s reply with an elliptical verb of speaking: literally, “And I [said].” The pronoun “io” is expressed for emphasis and to mark the change of speaker.
segments.inferno-7-g63: “Maestro” is a vocative, directly addressing Virgil as Dante’s guide and teacher. The comma marks the address before Dante’s actual question or statement begins.
segments.inferno-7-g64: “Tra questi cotali” means “among such people as these,” with “cotali” reinforcing the category just described. “Dovre’” is a poetic contraction of “dovrei,” the conditional “I should/ought to,” followed by the infinitive “riconoscere”; “furo” is an archaic form of “furono,” “they were.” The relative clause “che furo immondi di cotesti mali” defines “alcuni”: people who were morally polluted by the evils just mentioned.
segments.inferno-7-g65: “Ed elli a me” is another elliptical speech formula: “And he [said] to me.” “Elli” is the archaic Tuscan form of “egli,” meaning “he,” though the supplied sense list does not contain that meaning.
segments.inferno-7-g66: “Vano pensiero” is the direct object placed before the verb for emphasis: “a vain thought.” “Aduni” is second-person singular present, “you gather/assemble,” meaning that Dante is putting together a mistaken expectation in his mind.
segments.inferno-7-g67: “La sconoscente vita” is the subject: their ignorant or undiscerning way of life. “Che i fé sozzi” is a relative clause; “i” is an archaic object pronoun meaning “them,” and “fé” is a shortened preterite form of “fece,” “made.”
segments.inferno-7-g68: The same subject, “la sconoscente vita,” governs “fa”: the life that made them foul now makes them “bruni,” dark or obscure. “Ad ogne conoscenza” means “to every recognition,” i.e. unrecognizable to anyone; “li” is an object pronoun meaning “them,” not the article in this use.
segments.inferno-7-g69: “In etterno” uses the archaic spelling “etterno” and functions adverbially: “forever” or “for eternity.” “Verranno a li due cozzi” literally means “they will come to the two clashes,” with “a li” an older/uncontracted form equivalent to modern “ai.”
segments.inferno-7-g70: “Questi” points to one of the two groups being contrasted. “Resurgeranno” is future tense, “they will rise again/be resurrected,” and “del sepulcro” means “from the tomb”; “col pugno chiuso” describes them as rising with a clenched fist, a symbolic gesture of grasping or hoarding.
segments.inferno-7-g71: “E questi” introduces the other contrasted group, parallel to the previous “questi.” “Coi crin mozzi” means “with hair cut off/shorn,” where “crin” is a poetic singular or collective form for “hair.”
segments.inferno-7-g72: “Mal dare e mal tener” are two substantivized infinitive phrases, “bad giving and bad keeping,” serving together as the subject. “Ha tolto” is singular because the paired actions are treated as one cause; “lo mondo pulcro” is the direct object, “the beautiful world,” and “loro” is an indirect pronoun meaning “from them/for them” in sense.
segments.inferno-7-g73: “E posti” continues the effect of the same subject, with the auxiliary understood from “ha”: “and [has] put/set them.” “A questa zuffa” means “to this brawl,” indicating the punishment in which they are fixed.
segments.inferno-7-g74: “Qual ella sia” is an indirect or parenthetical clause with the subjunctive “sia”: “what sort of thing it may be.” “Ella” refers back to “questa zuffa,” but in English it is naturally rendered as “it.”
segments.inferno-7-g75: “Parole non ci appulcro” has an unusual verb, “appulcro,” first-person singular present: “I adorn/beautify.” “Ci” is adverbial, referring to the matter just mentioned, so the phrase means that Virgil will not dress up or embellish the description with fine words.
segments.inferno-7-g47: “E io” resumes the narrative with “And I,” setting up Dante as the subject of the main verb “dissi” that comes after the relative clause. The pronoun “io” is emphatic because the intervening clause describes his emotional state before he speaks.
segments.inferno-7-g48: “ch’avea” is “che avea,” with the relative pronoun elided before a vowel: “who/which had.” “Avea” is an archaic imperfect form of “aveva,” and “lo cor” is poetic for “il cuore”; “quasi compunto” means his heart was almost pricked or pierced emotionally.
segments.inferno-7-g49: “Dissi” is the first-person singular past tense, “I said.” It is the main verb whose subject was introduced in “E io.”
segments.inferno-7-g50: “Maestro mio” is a direct address to Virgil, with the possessive adjective following the noun, a normal Italian order especially in vocatives. It marks respect and dependence on Virgil as guide and teacher.
segments.inferno-7-g51: “Or” means “now,” and “mi dimostra” means “show/explain to me,” with “mi” functioning as an indirect object. “Che gente è questa” is an indirect question: literally “what people is this,” meaning “what sort of people these are.”
segments.inferno-7-g52: Here “se” means “whether,” introducing a second indirect question dependent on “dimostra.” “Fuor” is not “outside” here but an archaic poetic form for “furono,” “were”; “chercuti” means tonsured, referring to the shaved crowns of clerics, and “a la sinistra nostra” means “on our left.”
segments.inferno-7-g53: “Ed elli a me” is an elliptical speech formula: literally “And he to me,” meaning “And he said to me.” “Elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” “he,” referring to Virgil.
segments.inferno-7-g54: “Tutti quanti” is an emphatic phrase meaning “all of them, every one.” Again “fuor” is an archaic form of “furono,” “were”; “guerci de la mente” is metaphorical, saying that their minds were squinting, crooked, or distorted during their former earthly life.
segments.inferno-7-g55: The “che” completes the correlative construction “sì ... che,” meaning “so ... that.” “Con misura” means “with moderation,” while “nullo spendio ferci” uses “nullo” for “no” and “ferci,” an archaic form of “fecero,” meaning “they made”: they never spent in measured proportion.
segments.inferno-7-g56: “La voce lor” has poetic word order for “la loro voce,” “their voice.” “Chiaro” functions adverbially, “clearly,” and “l’abbaia” contains the object pronoun “lo,” “it,” before the verb: their shouting or barking makes the fact clear.
segments.inferno-7-g57: “Vegnono” is an archaic form of “vengono,” “they come.” “A’ due punti” contracts “ai due punti,” “to the two points,” and the relative clause “dove colpa contraria li dispaia” explains that at those points their opposite sins separate them into opposing groups.
segments.inferno-7-g58: “Questi” means “these people,” referring to the tonsured figures Dante noticed. “Fuor” is again the archaic poetic form of “furono,” “were,” so the phrase identifies them as clerics.
segments.inferno-7-g59: “Che” is a relative pronoun, “who,” referring to the clerics. “Non han” is the shortened form of “non hanno,” and “coperchio piloso al capo” literally means “a hairy covering on the head,” a periphrasis for hair, absent because of the clerical tonsure.
segments.inferno-7-g60: The repeated “e ... e ...” gives a cumulative or “both ... and ...” force: not merely ordinary clerics, but also popes and cardinals. “Papi” here means “popes,” despite the misleading lexical form in the word list.
segments.inferno-7-g61: “In cui” means “in whom,” referring back to popes and cardinals. “Usa” here means “practices” or “exercises,” with “avarizia” as the subject and “il suo soperchio” as the object: avarice carried its excess to the full in them.
segments.inferno-7-g92: Or means “now,” introducing Virgil’s corrective explanation. Vo’ is an apocopated form of voglio, “I want,” governing che + subjunctive: tu ... ’mbocche, “that you take into your mouth/take in”; mia sentenza is the object placed before the verb, and ne functions with ’mbocche as an archaic/poetic form suggesting “into” or “thereof/about it.”
segments.inferno-7-g93: Colui introduces the subject, “He who,” referring to God. Lo cui is an archaic possessive-relative construction meaning “whose,” and saver is a substantivized infinitive, “knowing/wisdom”; tutto is the object of trascende, “surpasses everything.”
segments.inferno-7-g94: Fece and diè are preterites: “made” and “gave.” Li cieli is archaic for i cieli, “the heavens/celestial spheres,” while lor means “to them”; chi conduce is a relative-like expression, “one who guides” or “those who guide,” and sì anticipates the following ch’, forming “so ... that.”
segments.inferno-7-g95: Ch’ continues the correlative sì ... che, “so that.” Ogne is an archaic form of ogni, “every,” and the repeated phrase ogne parte ad ogne parte means “each part to every other part”; splende is present indicative, “shines.”
segments.inferno-7-g96: Distribuendo is a gerund modifying the preceding action: the heavenly order shines by “distributing” light. Igualmente means “equally” or “uniformly,” and la luce is the direct object of the gerund.
segments.inferno-7-g97: Similemente links this sentence to the previous one: just as God ordered the heavens, he also ordered earthly affairs. Ordinò is the main preterite verb, with general ministra e duce as its object, referring to Fortune as a universal minister and guide; che permutasse is an imperfect subjunctive dependent on ordinò, expressing the role she was ordained to perform. A tempo means “at the proper time,” li ben vani means “the vain/fleeting goods,” and the paired phrases di gente in gente and d’uno in altro sangue express transfer “from nation to nation” and “from one lineage to another.”
segments.inferno-7-g98: Oltre means “beyond,” introducing a limit that human beings cannot pass. La difension d’i senni umani means “the defense/resistance of human minds or reason,” with d’i an archaic contraction of di + i; the phrase says Fortune’s operations lie beyond human intellectual control or protection.
segments.inferno-7-g99: Per ch’ forms perché/per che in the sense “wherefore,” “for which reason,” or “therefore.” Una gente and l’altra are balanced subjects, while impera and langue are present indicatives describing the alternating results of Fortune’s rule: one people rules, the other declines.
segments.inferno-7-g100: Seguendo is a gerund, “following,” explaining how the rise and fall of peoples happens. Lo giudicio is an archaic form of il giudizio, “the judgment,” and di costei means “of this woman/this one,” referring to Fortune, though the supplied glosses for costei do not match the feminine use here.
segments.inferno-7-g101: Che is a relative pronoun referring most naturally to giudicio, “judgment”: her judgment is hidden. Occulto is a predicate adjective after è, and come in erba l’angue is a simile, “like the serpent in the grass,” with the subject l’angue placed after the prepositional phrase for poetic effect.
segments.inferno-7-g102: Vostro saver uses saver as a noun, “your knowledge/understanding.” Non ha contasto a lei literally means “has no opposition to her,” that is, human knowledge cannot resist or contend with Fortune; a lei is the indirect/prepositional complement, “against/to her.”
segments.inferno-7-g103: Questa means “this one,” again referring to Fortune. Provede is present indicative; in context it means that she provides for, oversees, or administers events according to her office.
segments.inferno-7-g104: Giudica is another present indicative coordinated with provede and persegue. It states one of Fortune’s governing functions: she “judges” or decides the distribution of worldly goods.
segments.inferno-7-g105: E persegue continues the list of present-tense verbs: Fortune “pursues” or carries out the administration of suo regno, “her realm.” Come introduces a comparison; il loro means “their own realm,” with regno understood, and li altri dèi is the postposed subject, “the other gods.”
segments.inferno-7-g106: “Le sue permutazion” is the plural subject, referring to Fortune’s continual changes; “permutazion” is a poetic truncated plural for “permutazioni.” “Non hanno triegue” literally says they “have no truces,” meaning they never pause or rest.
segments.inferno-7-g107: “Necessità” is the subject and is almost personified as the force that drives Fortune. “La fa esser veloce” uses “fare” plus infinitive: literally “makes her be swift,” with “la” as the direct object referring back to Fortune.
segments.inferno-7-g108: “Sì spesso” means “so often,” introducing the reason for her speed. “Vien chi vicenda consegue” has poetic word order: literally “comes he who obtains a turn,” meaning new people constantly arrive to take their allotted change of fortune.
segments.inferno-7-g109: “Quest’ è colei ch’...” means “This is she/the one who...,” with elision in “Quest’” and “ch’.” “È posta in croce” is a passive construction, literally “is put on the cross,” used figuratively for being harshly attacked or blamed; “pur da color” means “even by those,” and “dovrien” is an archaic conditional form meaning “should.”
segments.inferno-7-g110: “Dandole” is a gerund with an attached dative pronoun: “giving to her.” “A torto” is an idiom meaning “wrongly” or “unjustly,” and “mala voce” means a bad reputation or bad name rather than merely a bad sound.
segments.inferno-7-g111: “Ma” turns from human slander to Fortune’s own blessed state. “Ella s’è beata” uses an archaic/reflexive phrasing, essentially “she is blessed in herself,” and “ciò non ode” means she does not hear that blame.
segments.inferno-7-g112: “Con l’altre prime creature” places Fortune among the first-created beings, traditionally angelic intelligences. “Lieta” agrees with the feminine subject, while “volve sua spera” means she turns her celestial sphere; “si gode” is reflexive/medio-passive in feel, “rejoices in herself.”
segments.inferno-7-g113: “Or” and “omai” together give the sense “now, at last” or “by now.” “Discendiamo” is first-person plural present used hortatively, “let us descend,” and “a maggior pieta” means toward a place of greater pity or wretched suffering.
segments.inferno-7-g114: “Già” marks the passage of time: the stars that were rising are now falling. “Che saliva” is a relative clause, “which was rising,” and “quand’ io mi mossi” means “when I set out,” with “mi mossi” as a reflexive-like idiom for beginning to move.
segments.inferno-7-g115: “’l” is the elided article “il.” “Il troppo star” nominalizes the infinitive “stare,” meaning “too much staying” or “too long a delay,” and “si vieta” is impersonal/passive in sense: “is forbidden” or “one must not.”
segments.inferno-7-g116: “Noi ricidemmo il cerchio” literally says “we cut the circle again,” a poetic way of saying they crossed or cut across the circular path. “A l’altra riva” gives their destination, “to the other bank,” while the relative clauses describe the spring: it boils, pours forth through a ditch, and that ditch derives or flows from the spring itself, “da lei.”
segments.inferno-7-g131: “Lo” is an archaic form of the masculine definite article before “buon maestro,” meaning “the good master,” i.e. Virgil. “Disse” is the passato remoto of “dire,” the normal narrative past tense here: “said.”
segments.inferno-7-g132: “Figlio” is a direct vocative: Virgil addresses Dante affectionately as “son” or “my son.” It stands outside the syntax of the following command.
segments.inferno-7-g133: “Or” means “now,” and “vedi” is present indicative, functioning almost like an imperative: “now see.” “Color” is a poetic shortened form of “coloro,” “those,” and “cui” is the object of “vinse”: literally “those whom anger overcame.”
segments.inferno-7-g134: “Vo’” is not the verb “to go” here but a contracted poetic form of “voglio,” “I want”; the supplied sense list does not include that value. “Per certo” is an adverbial phrase meaning “for certain,” and the following “che” introduces what Dante is to believe: that there are sighing souls under the water.
segments.inferno-7-g135: “Fanno pullular” is a causative construction: “they make bubble/spring up.” “Quest’ acqua” is elided before a vowel, and “al summo” means “to/on the top,” that is, to the surface of the water.
segments.inferno-7-g136: “Come” means “as,” introducing visual evidence for the claim just made. “L’occhio ti dice” personifies the eye as something that “tells you,” with “ti” as the indirect object.
segments.inferno-7-g137: “U’” is a poetic contraction for “ove,” meaning “where.” Together with “che,” it has the force of “wherever,” and “s’aggira” is reflexive/intransitive: the eye “turns” or “ranges about.”
segments.inferno-7-g138: “Fitti nel limo” is a fronted participial phrase, literally “stuck in the mud,” describing the souls before the main verb. “Dicon” is a shortened poetic form of “dicono,” “they say.”
segments.inferno-7-g139: “Tristi fummo” places the adjective first for emphasis: “gloomy/sullen we were.” “Ne l’aere” is the older separated spelling of modern “nell’aere,” and “che dal sol s’allegra” means “which is gladdened by the sun”; “sol” here is poetic for “sole,” not “only.”
segments.inferno-7-g140: “Portando” is a gerund explaining the condition in which they lived: while carrying something within. “Dentro” means “within,” and “accidïoso fummo” means a smoke of accidia, spiritual sloth or sullen gloom, inside them.
segments.inferno-7-g141: “Or” contrasts their present punishment with their former life: “now.” “Ci attristiam” is a poetic shortened form of “ci attristiamo,” reflexive, “we make ourselves sad / we grow sorrowful,” and “ne la belletta negra” is the older spelling of “nella,” “in the black mire.”
segments.inferno-7-g142: “Quest’ inno” is the object placed first for emphasis: “this hymn.” “Si gorgoglian” is a poetic plural form with reflexive “si,” meaning that they gurgle it to themselves/in themselves, and “ne la strozza” is old spelling for “nella strozza,” “in the throat.”
segments.inferno-7-g143: “Ché” gives the reason: “because/for.” “Nol” contracts “non lo,” “not it,” referring back to the hymn, and “posson” is a shortened form of “possono”; “con parola integra” means “with a complete, intact word,” because the mud-choked souls cannot articulate clearly.
segments.inferno-7-g144: “Così” links the action to what has just been described: “thus/so.” “Girammo” is passato remoto of “girare,” “we went around,” and “grand’ arco de la lorda pozza” means that they traced a large arc along the filthy pool, between the dry bank and the middle.
segments.inferno-7-g145: “Con li occhi vòlti” uses the older article “li” and a past participle: “with the eyes turned.” “A chi” means “toward those who,” and “del fango ingozza” uses a partitive “del,” literally “gorges on some of the mud.”

sentences.inferno-7-s2: Plutus began in his hoarse voice; and that noble sage, who knew everything, said to comfort me: “Let your fear not harm you; for, whatever power he may have, he will not prevent our descending this rock.”
sentences.inferno-7-s3: Then he turned toward that swollen face and said: “Be silent, accursed wolf!”
sentences.inferno-7-s4: Consume yourself within, with your own rage.
sentences.inferno-7-s13: They struck against one another; and then, right there, each one turned back, wheeling around, shouting, “Why do you hoard?”
sentences.inferno-7-s16: Thus they kept returning through the gloomy circle from every side to the opposite point, shouting at one another their shameful refrain; then each one turned back, when he had arrived, through his own half-circle to the other joust.
sentences.inferno-7-s9: Who heaps up so many new torments and punishments as I saw?
sentences.inferno-7-s10: And why does our guilt so ruin us?
sentences.inferno-7-s11: As the wave does there above Charybdis, which breaks against the one into which it runs, so here the people must whirl round.
sentences.inferno-7-s12: Here I saw a crowd greater than elsewhere, far too large, on one side and on the other, with great howls, rolling weights by the strength of their chests.
sentences.inferno-7-s5: Our going down into the depths is not without cause: it is willed on high, there where Michael took retribution for the proud outrage.
sentences.inferno-7-s6: As wind-swollen sails fall entangled when the mast breaks, so the cruel beast fell to the ground.
sentences.inferno-7-s7: Thus we descended into the fourth hollow, going farther along the sorrowful bank that encloses all the evil of the universe.
sentences.inferno-7-s45: At last we came to the foot of a tower.
sentences.inferno-7-s25: Now, my son, you can see the brief jest of the goods that are entrusted to Fortune, over which humankind wrangles; for all the gold that is beneath the moon, and that ever was, could not make one of these weary souls rest.
sentences.inferno-7-s26: “My master,” I said, “now tell me also: this Fortune of which you speak to me, what is she, who so holds the goods of the world in her claws?”
sentences.inferno-7-s28: And he to me: “Oh foolish creatures, how great is the ignorance that harms you!”
sentences.inferno-7-s37: The water was far darker than dark purple; and we, in company with the murky waves, entered downward along a different path.
sentences.inferno-7-s38: This wretched little stream goes into the marsh that is called Styx, once it has descended to the foot of the malignant gray slopes.
sentences.inferno-7-s39: And I, who was intent on gazing, saw muddy people in that marsh, all naked, with an injured expression.
sentences.inferno-7-s40: These struck one another not only with their hands, but with their heads and with their chests and with their feet, tearing one another with their teeth piece by piece.
sentences.inferno-7-s21: And I: “Master, among such as these I should surely recognize some who were defiled by those evils.”
sentences.inferno-7-s22: And he to me: “You gather a vain thought: the undiscerning life that made them foul now makes them dark to all recognition.”
sentences.inferno-7-s23: For eternity they will come to the two collisions: these will rise again from the tomb with clenched fist, and these with shorn hair.
sentences.inferno-7-s24: Bad giving and bad keeping have taken the beautiful world from them and set them to this brawl: what sort of thing it is, I do not embellish with words.
sentences.inferno-7-s17: And I, whose heart was almost pierced, said: “My master, now show me what people these are, and whether all these tonsured ones on our left were clerics.”
sentences.inferno-7-s18: And he said to me: “All of them were so warped in mind in the first life that they made no expenditure with moderation.”
sentences.inferno-7-s19: Their voice barks it out quite clearly when they come to the two points of the circle where opposite guilt separates them.
sentences.inferno-7-s20: These were clerics, who have no hairy covering on their heads, and popes and cardinals, in whom avarice practices its excess.”
sentences.inferno-7-s29: Now I want you to take in my judgment about it.
sentences.inferno-7-s30: He whose wisdom surpasses everything made the heavens and gave them those who guide them, so that every part shines upon every part, distributing the light equally.
sentences.inferno-7-s31: Likewise, for worldly splendors, he ordained a general minister and leader who should change the vain goods from people to people and from one lineage to another at the proper time, beyond the defense of human understanding; whereby one nation rules and another declines, following the judgment of this one, which is hidden like a serpent in the grass.
sentences.inferno-7-s32: Your knowledge has no power to oppose her: she provides, judges, and governs her realm as the other gods do theirs.
sentences.inferno-7-s33: Her changes have no respite: necessity makes her swift; so often comes one who obtains a turn.
sentences.inferno-7-s34: This is she who is so much put on the cross even by those who ought to give her praise, giving her blame unjustly and a bad name; but she is blessed and does not hear it: joyful with the other first-created beings, she turns her sphere and, blessed, rejoices in herself.
sentences.inferno-7-s35: Now let us descend at last to greater pity; already every star is falling that was rising when I set out, and too long a stay is forbidden.
sentences.inferno-7-s36: We cut across the circle to the other bank, above a spring that boils and pours out through a ditch that flows from it.
sentences.inferno-7-s41: The good master said: “Son, now you see the souls of those whom anger overcame; and I also want you to believe for certain that beneath the water there are people who sigh, and they make this water bubble up to the surface, as your eye tells you wherever it turns.”
sentences.inferno-7-s42: Stuck in the mud they say: “Gloomy we were in the sweet air that is gladdened by the sun, carrying slothful smoke within us; now we grow sorrowful in the black mire.”
sentences.inferno-7-s43: This hymn they gurgle in their throats, because they cannot say it with a whole word.”
sentences.inferno-7-s44: Thus we went around a great arc of the filthy pool, between the dry bank and the middle, with our eyes turned toward those who gulp down the mud.
